title: Scheduling features needed for UVM support
body:
### Introduction

Our goal is to be able to use UVM with Verilator.

Note that we completely ignore the fact that currently the whole UVM cannot even be parsed by Verilator, let's focus on scheduling here.
The rest is a part of a separate effort.

There are two significant things that need addressing:
* lack of support for the `fork`/`join` statements
* lack of events triggering (`->my_event`) and waiting for them inside tasks (`@ my_event`)

The ideal way to solve this would be jump right into handling event driven simulation and implementing a _full_ stratified scheduler.

We want to discuss other possible ways to implement what is needed.
Maybe it would be possible to make the first implementation a little bit more simplified.
Below are some notes on various approaches that could be taken.

### Execute forked code sequentially

The idea here is that maybe we could execute the following code:

```verilog
fork
  <block A>
  <block B>
  <block C>
join
```

in sequence (i.e. execute A then B then C - not in parallel).

We would probably have to show a warning when doing that as not everything would work as expected with this approach.

Advantages:
* the biggest benefit of this would be that it would be the easiest to implement
* would allow us to move forward with the implementation of stuff missing for UVM

Disadvantages:
* will work with a limited number of cases
* will have to be re-visited sooner or later

This is also how regular blocks (`initial` or block reacting to the same edge) are used.

### Combining `fork`/`join` with `#delay` timing

Assuming https://github.com/verilator/verilator/pull/2409 - or some other implementation goes to master, the approach described above would have to be different.

Each forked block would have to be represented as a separate FSM (similarly to what the code in the branch does for each `initial` block, etc.).
The new FSM would have to be nested in the ones handling the code where the `fork` statement is used.

### Handling event triggers

There are various ways the events can be used.
However the way UVM uses events in most cases is fairly simple, here is an example:

```systemverilog
virtual task wait_trigger ();
  num_waiters++;
  @m_event;
endtask
```

So basically this boils down to blocking the execution until somebody triggers the event with `->m_event`.

The simplest way to implement this would be using a FSM mechanism similar to #2409.
Calling `->` could advance the state in the machine, or maybe just set some boolean flag that would allow the machine to advance itself during the next eval loop.


We also need to support the `.triggered` method for events.
It should be fairly easy to add a special case to handle that.

### Implementation of a proper stratified scheduler

This would probably be the most preferable way as this would be the closest to what should be done according to the IEEE.
There are many regions that could be ignored in the first implementation (namely the PLI-only regions).

All regions:
* Preponed
* Pre-Active
* Active
* Inactive
* Pre-NBA
* NBA
* Post-NBA
* Pre-Observed
* Observed
* Post-Observed
* Reactive
* Re-Inactive
* Pre-Re-NBA
* Re-NBA
* Post-Re-NBA
* Pre-Postpone
* Postponed

PLI specific regions which can be ignored for now:
* Preponed
* Pre-Active
* Pre-NBA
* Post-NBA
* Pre-Observed
* Post-Observed
* Pre-Re-NBA
* Post-Re-NBA
* Pre-Postponed

So we are left with 8 regions:
* Active
* Inactive
* NBA
* Observed
* Reactive
* Re-Inactive
* Re-NBA
* Postponed

Implementing the stratified scheduler would still be the most time consuming approach.
The main issue with that is it would have to modify lots of the features that are already supported as even assignments would have to go through the queue.

We are willing to start our implementation with the simple approaches suggested above.
If we find some blockers or come up with new ideas during our work we will try to keep this updated.

We create this issue here before starting our work so you can share your comments if there is some early feedback or some blockers we did not anticipate or if there is something fundamentally wrong with our approaches.
Please do so!

@wsnyder when we talked about this some time ago you mentioned that this will not be doable without using actual threads for the implementation.
Could you please elaborate on that?
